content.problemStatement:
Chef Ada constructed a simple undirected graph GGG with NNN vertices (numbered 111 through NNN) as follows:


Start with a complete graph with KKK vertices and K⋅(K−1)/2K \cdot (K-1)/2K⋅(K−1)/2 edges.
Then, add N−KN-KN−K vertices one by one. After adding each vetex, find some clique of K−1K-1K−1 vertices in the graph and connect the new vertex to all the vertices of that clique.
Consider NNN boolean variables X1,X2,…,XNX_1, X_2, \ldots, X_NX1​,X2​,…,XN​ and their negations ¬X1,¬X2,…,¬XN\neg X_1, \neg X_2, \ldots, \neg X_N¬X1​,¬X2​,…,¬XN​, corresponding to vertices of the graph. You are given the initial values of all variables.

A CNF boolean expression is defined as a conjunction of clauses, where each clause is a disjunction of two or more elements of the set of variables and their negations.

Using the graph GGG, Ada wrote a CNF boolean expression BBB with MMM clauses, where each clause corresponds to a clique in the graph with some (maybe none) of the variables (corresponding to vertices) negated. More formally, for each iii from 111 to MMM:


Ada chose some clique in the graph, consisting of LiL_iLi​ vertices Z1,Z2,…,ZLiZ_1, Z_2, \ldots, Z_{L_i}Z1​,Z2​,…,ZLi​​
then for each valid jjj, Ada chose a variable Pi,jP_{i,j}Pi,j​ as either XZjX_{Z_j}XZj​​ or ¬XZj\neg X_{Z_j}¬XZj​​
the iii-th clause in the expression BBB had LiL_iLi​ terms: Pi,1∨Pi,2∨…∨Pi,LiP_{i,1} \lor P_{i,2} \lor \ldots \lor P_{i,L_i}Pi,1​∨Pi,2​∨…∨Pi,Li​​
Ada wants the expression to evaluate to "true". In order to achieve that, she may change the values of variables; for each valid iii, the cost of changing the value of XiX_iXi​ (from "true" to "false" or vice versa) is CiC_iCi​. Find the minimum total cost needed to make the expression BBB evaluate to "true" or determine that it is impossible.
constraints: each character of SSS is either '0' or '1'
1≤Li≤N1 \le L_i \le N1≤Li​≤N for each valid iii
Subtasks**Subtask #1 (25 points, time limit 1 second):** K=3K = 3K=3
content.inputFormat:
The first line of the input contains a single integer TTT denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.
The first line of each test case contains three space-separated integers NNN, MMM and KKK.
The second line contains a single binary string SSS with length NNN describing the initial values of the variables. For each valid iii, the iii-th character of SSS is '1' if XiX_iXi​ is true or '0' if XiX_iXi​ is false.
The third line contains NNN space-separated integers C1,C2,…,CNC_1, C_2, \ldots, C_NC1​,C2​,…,CN​.
MMM lines follow. For each valid iii, the iii-th of these lines contains an integer LiL_iLi​ followed by a space and LiL_iLi​ space-separated integers representing the variables Pi,1,…,Pi,LiP_{i,1}, \ldots, P_{i,L_i}Pi,1​,…,Pi,Li​​. For each valid iii, the variable XiX_iXi​ is represented by the integer iii and its negation is represented by −i-i−i.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print a single line containing one integer ― the minimum cost needed to make the boolean expression evaluate to "true", or −1-1−1 if it is impossible.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Example case 1: The following figure depicts the graph used to generate the expression.

The boolean expression is B=(X1∨¬X2∨X4)∧(X2∨X3∨¬X5)∧(X1∨¬X3∨X2)B = (X_1 \lor \neg X_2 \lor X_4) \land (X_2 \lor X_3 \lor \neg X_5) \land (X_1 \lor \neg X_3 \lor X_2)B=(X1​∨¬X2​∨X4​)∧(X2​∨X3​∨¬X5​)∧(X1​∨¬X3​∨X2​).
One optimal solution is to change the values of X1X_1X1​ and X3X_3X3​.
